content.text:
```json
{
  "module": "database-api",
  "scores": {
    "visual_polish": 35,
    "domain_depth": 58,
    "data_richness": 52,
    "interaction_quality": 45,
    "ai_integration": 30,
    "mobile_first": 25,
    "performance": 55,
    "typescript_quality": 62,
    "error_handling": 58,
    "realtime": 50,
    "accessibility": 20,
    "security": 55,
    "differentiation": 35,
    "enterprise_readiness": 48
  },
  "overall_score": 45,
  "issues": [
    {
      "id": "database-api-C2-001",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "error_handling",
      "title": "getProject throws raw Supabase errors without user-friendly messages",
      "file": "src/api/endpoints/projects.ts",
      "description": "getProject calls transformSupabaseError but the ApiError constructor for missing projectId uses a hardcoded string that may not display well. More critically, fetchScheduleMetrics silently returns nulls on error without logging, making debugging schedule calculation failures impossible. The parallel Promise.all in getProject will reject if either promise rejects, but fetchScheduleMetrics swallows its own errors, creating inconsistent behavior.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/api/endpoints/projects.ts, in the fetchScheduleMetrics function: BEFORE: The function returns { scheduleVarianceDays: null, completionPercentage: null } on error with no logging or context. The error path `if (error || !data || data.length === 0)` treats a database error the same as empty data. AFTER: Split the error case from the empty data case. If `error` is truthy, log it via `console.warn('fetchScheduleMetrics failed for project', projectId, error.message)` and return nulls. If `!data || data.length === 0` (no error, just no phases), return nulls silently. This preserves the non-throwing contract but gives operators visibility into failures. Also add a return type annotation `Promise<{ scheduleVarianceDays: number | null; completionPercentage: number | null }>` to the function signature for clarity. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "database-api-C2-002",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "typescript_quality",
      "title": "buildPaginatedQuery generic usage lacks type safety for transform function",
      "file": "src/api/endpoints/people.ts",
      "description": "getDirectory and getMeetings use buildPaginatedQuery with inline transform functions that add properties (contactName, companyGroup, attendeeCount, etc.) but the row type coming from Supabase may not match the expected shape. The `d.name`, `d.company`, `d.date`, `d.notes` property accesses assume specific column names exist without validation. If the database schema changes, these will fail silently at runtime returning undefined values rather than TypeScript catching the mismatch at compile time.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/api/endpoints/people.ts, in the getDirectory function's transform callback: BEFORE: The transform accesses `d.name`, `d.company` directly from the row type `DirectoryContactRow` without null checks, and the `contactName` field is typed as `string | null` but `d.name` could be undefined if the column is missing from the select. AFTER: Add explicit null coalescing to the transform: `contactName: d.name ?? null` and `companyGroup: d.company ?? 'Unaffiliated'`. In getMeetings transform, change `time: m.date ? new Date(m.date).toLocaleTimeString(...)  : ''` to also guard against invalid date strings: `time: m.date && !isNaN(new Date(m.date).getTime()) ? new Date(m.date).toLocaleTimeString('en-US', { hour: 'numeric', minute: '2-digit' }) : ''`. This prevents runtime NaN/Invalid Date from propagating to the UI. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "database-api-C2-003",
      "severity": "critical",
      "dimension": "security",
      "title": "assertProjectAccess dedup cache could serve stale membership data after role revocation",
      "file": "src/api/middleware/projectScope.ts",
      "description": "assertProjectAccess uses dedupTtl with a 30-second TTL to cache project membership checks. If an admin revokes a user's project access, the user retains access for up to 30 seconds due to the cache. For security-sensitive operations (budget edits, pay app submissions), this is a P0 issue. The cache key includes user_id and project_id but not a cache-bust signal from role changes.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/api/middleware/projectScope.ts, in the assertProjectAccess function: BEFORE: The membership check uses `dedupTtl(key, 30000, ...)` which caches the result for 30 seconds. This means a revoked user can still pass access checks for up to 30 seconds after revocation. AFTER: Reduce the TTL from 30000ms to 5000ms (5 seconds) to minimize the window of stale access. Add a comment: `// TTL kept short (5s) to minimize stale access window after role revocation. // For write operations on sensitive entities (budget, pay apps), consider bypassing cache entirely.` This is the minimal surgical fix. A full solution would involve cache invalidation on role changes via realtime subscription, but that is a new feature. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "database-api-C2-004",
      "severity": "major",
      "dimension": "realtime",
      "title": "Activity feed enrichment makes N+1 queries for entity labels without batching",
      "file": "src/api/endpoints/activity.ts",
      "description": "getActivityFeed fetches 50 activity items then calls `enrichActivityItem` on each via `Promise.all`, where each enrichment calls `fetchEntityLabel` which makes a separate Supabase query per entity. For a full activity feed this means up to 50 additional database round trips. The entity label cache helps on subsequent loads but the first load is extremely slow.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/api/endpoints/activity.ts, in the getActivityFeed function: BEFORE: `return Promise.all((data || []).map((item) => enrichActivityItem(item as ActivityFeedRowWithProfile, projectId)))` makes up to 50 sequential-via-parallel queries to fetch entity labels. AFTER: Before the Promise.all, collect all unique entity IDs grouped by type from the data array. Then batch-fetch labels for each type in a single query. Add a helper function `async function batchFetchEntityLabels(items: ActivityFeedRowWithProfile[], projectId: string): Promise<Map<string, string>>` that: 1) Groups items by entityType, 2) For each type (rfi, submittal, etc.), runs ONE `.in('id', ids)` query, 3) Returns a Map<entityId, label>. Then modify enrichActivityItem to accept this pre-fetched map as an optional parameter and use it instead of calling fetchEntityLabel individually. This reduces N+1 to at most 5-6 queries (one per entity type). After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    },
    {
      "id": "database-api-C2-005",
      "severity": "major",
      "dimension": "enterprise_readiness",
      "title": "getPortfolioMetrics lacks fallback when RPC function does not exist",
      "file": "src/api/endpoints/organizations.ts",
      "description": "getPortfolioMetrics calls an RPC function `get_portfolio_metrics` that may not exist in the database (the migration may not have been applied). If the RPC call fails, the function throws transformSupabaseError which surfaces a raw Postgres error to the UI. The ZERO_METRICS constant is defined but never used as a fallback. Enterprise users hitting this on first deploy see a cryptic error instead of zeros.",
      "prompt": "In the file src/api/endpoints/organizations.ts, in the getPortfolioMetrics function: BEFORE: The function defines `ZERO_METRICS` as a constant but the actual RPC call and subsequent queries throw on error without falling back to it. If `get_portfolio_metrics` RPC does not exist, the user sees a raw database error. AFTER: Wrap the RPC call in a try-catch. If the RPC fails (e.g., function does not exist, indicated by error code `42883` or any error), log a warning `console.warn('get_portfolio_metrics RPC unavailable, computing from queries:', error.message)` and fall back to computing metrics from direct table queries (count projects, sum contract_values). If even the fallback queries fail, return the ZERO_METRICS object with all zero values. This ensures the portfolio dashboard always renders, even on fresh deployments where migrations are incomplete. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors."
    }
  ],
  "invented_features": [],
  "competitive_intel": "Procore's API layer uses GraphQL with automatic batching and field-level caching, which avoids the N+1 problem SiteSync has in its activity feed enrichment. Fieldwire uses aggressive client-side caching with background sync. SiteSync's middleware pattern (assertProjectAccess with dedup) is architecturally sound but the 30-second cache TTL is a security liability that Procore avoids by using JWT claims for access checks rather than per-request DB queries. The typed Supabase client approach is modern and competitive, but the lack of batch query patterns puts SiteSync behind on performance for data-heavy pages like Dashboard and Portfolio.",
  "summary": "The Database & API Layer has solid architectural foundations: typed Supabase client, middleware-based access control, real-time subscriptions, and structured error transformation. However, five surgical issues hold it back. The security-critical membership cache TTL is too long (30s), risking stale access after revocation. The activity feed makes N+1 queries that will cripple dashboard performance at scale. Project schedule metric errors are silently swallowed, making debugging impossible. Date parsing in people endpoints lacks guards against invalid values. Portfolio metrics have no graceful fallback when the expected RPC function is missing. Fixing these five issues would move the score from 45 to approximately 60, bringing error handling, security, performance, and enterprise readiness to acceptable levels for a GC managing a $50M project."
}
```
